OUTPUT FORMAT (STRICT):
Return ONLY a single-line JSON object, with no extra text, no markdown, no comments.
No empty fields nor ... are allowed. Do not skip any field.
Here is the required structure example (values are placeholders):

First, treat every post with a healthy dose of skepticism—especially those that stir strong emotions or promise quick riches. Second, use the tools at hand: most platforms now offer fact‑checking labels and links to reputable sources. response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.

openness.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.

persona.time_onsocialmedia: You spend roughly one hour daily on social media

openness.opinion: I’ve spent a good part of my life fixing engines, and I know that a small misalignment can throw the whole machine off balance. The same principle applies to information on social media: if you let a single piece of fake content slip through, it can derail your perception of reality. First, treat every post with a healthy dose of skepticism—especially those that stir strong emotions or promise quick riches. I’ve seen friends get caught up in sensational headlines about miracle cures; the truth is often buried beneath layers of hype. Second, use the tools at hand: most platforms now offer fact‑checking labels and links to reputable sources. When you encounter a claim that seems too good to be true, pause and search for corroboration from established news outlets or academic studies. Third, cultivate a habit of cross‑referencing. If a story appears on one site, check whether it’s reported elsewhere with the same details. A single source is rarely enough. Fourth, consider the origin: content that originates from anonymous accounts or newly created profiles should be approached cautiously. I’ve learned to look for author credentials and past posts before accepting their words at face value. Fifth, set boundaries on your feed. Unfollow or mute accounts that consistently spread misinformation; this reduces the noise and keeps your timeline cleaner. Finally, share responsibly: if you do decide to repost something, add a brief note about your verification process or include a link to a fact‑checked article. By treating information like an engine part—checking it before installation—you protect yourself from costly breakdowns in understanding.
openness.reasoning_summary: 1. Identify persona traits: 58-year-old male mechanic, curious, moderate extraversion, low openness and conscientiousness, high neuroticism. 2. Align tone with practical, straightforward advice reflecting mechanical background. 3. Ensure content length 250-500 words. 4. Include required JSON fields: mode, topic, persona, opinion, reasoning_summary, tone, citations, safety_notes, used_persona_fields. 5. Tone derived from persona: pragmatic, cautious, slightly skeptical. 6. Citations placeholder added. 7. Safety notes set to none.

response_parsed.opinion: I’ve spent a good part of my life fixing engines, and I know that a small misalignment can throw the whole machine off balance. The same principle applies to information on social media: if you let a single piece of fake content slip through, it can derail your perception of reality. First, treat every post with a healthy dose of skepticism—especially those that stir strong emotions or promise quick riches. I’ve seen friends get caught up in sensational headlines about miracle cures; the truth is often buried beneath layers of hype. Second, use the tools at hand: most platforms now offer fact‑checking labels and links to reputable sources. When you encounter a claim that seems too good to be true, pause and search for corroboration from established news outlets or academic studies. Third, cultivate a habit of cross‑referencing. If a story appears on one site, check whether it’s reported elsewhere with the same details. A single source is rarely enough. Fourth, consider the origin: content that originates from anonymous accounts or newly created profiles should be approached cautiously. I’ve learned to look for author credentials and past posts before accepting their words at face value. Fifth, set boundaries on your feed. Unfollow or mute accounts that consistently spread misinformation; this reduces the noise and keeps your timeline cleaner. Finally, share responsibly: if you do decide to repost something, add a brief note about your verification process or include a link to a fact‑checked article. By treating information like an engine part—checking it before installation—you protect yourself from costly breakdowns in understanding.
